mnemonics.title: Ford-Fulkerson Steps
mnemonics.content: FIND Residual → GET Augmenting Path → PUSH Bottleneck Flow → REPEAT until no path exists. Acronym: FRGP-PRN (Find Residual Get Path Push Repeat No path)

mnemonics.title: Flow Properties
mnemonics.content: CAPacity constraint: 0 to C (can't exceed). CONSERVation: IN = OUT (except s,t). Acronym: CC-C (Capacity Conservation - Complete)

mnemonics.title: Max-Flow Min-Cut
mnemonics.content: MAXimum FLOW = MINimum CUT. Both MAX and MIN start with M. M-M Rule: Match Max to Min.

acronyms.usage: The original algorithm for finding maximum flow in a network
Ford-Fulkerson implementation using BFS for polynomial time complexity
Graph traversal used in Edmonds-Karp to find shortest augmenting path
Partition of vertices separating source from sink; capacity is sum of forward edge capacities
Function representing available capacity in residual graph

visualTips.visualization: Think of it as a 'remaining capacity map'. Forward edges show how much MORE flow you can add; backward edges show how much flow you can UNDO. It's like a balance sheet showing available capacity.
Imagine water pipes with flow. An augmenting path is a route from source to sink where you can push more water. The bottleneck is the narrowest pipe limiting how much you can push.
Imagine cutting the network with scissors. The cut capacity is the total capacity of pipes you cut. The minimum cut is the weakest point separating source from sink.

Think of pipes meeting at a junction. Whatever flows in must flow out - like traffic at an intersection where cars entering must eventually exit (except source where flow originates and sink where it terminates).

analogies.analogy: Traffic network: Cars (flow) travel from home (source) to office (sink) through roads (edges) with capacity limits (lanes). Maximum flow is peak hour traffic volume without road congestion.
Gas tank capacity: Forward edges are empty space to add more fuel; backward edges are fuel already added that can be removed and rerouted.
Bottleneck in manufacturing: The weakest link in production determines maximum output. Like a factory assembly line where the slowest workstation limits overall production.
Chain strength: The strength of a chain is determined by its weakest link. Similarly, flow through a path is limited by the edge with minimum remaining capacity.
GPS navigation: Taking the shortest route (BFS) repeatedly is more efficient than randomly choosing routes, just as Edmonds-Karp guarantees better performance than basic Ford-Fulkerson.
